front: What are the 4 lobes of the cerebral cortex?
back: Frontal, Parietal, Temporal, Occipital

front: What is the function of the frontal lobe?
back: Motor function, executive planning, speech production (Broca), personality, decision-making

front: What is the function of the parietal lobe?
back: Sensory integration, spatial awareness, Wernicke area (language comprehension)

front: What is the blood supply of the anterior cerebral artery (ACA)?
back: Medial surface of frontal and parietal lobes, anterior 2/3 of corpus callosum, caudate head, internal capsule (anterior limb)

front: What is the blood supply of the middle cerebral artery (MCA)?
back: Lateral surface of frontal, parietal, temporal lobes; deep structures (putamen, globus pallidus, internal capsule)

front: Name the 3 arteries that supply the brainstem
back: Posterior cerebral artery (PCA), Basilar artery, Superior cerebellar artery (SCA)

front: What are the 3 subdivisions of the brainstem?
back: Midbrain, Pons, Medulla oblongata

front: What is the function of the cerebellum?
back: Motor coordination, balance, posture, motor learning, cognitive functions

front: What are the 3 peduncles of the cerebellum?
back: Superior cerebellar peduncle (output), Middle cerebellar peduncle (input from pons), Inferior cerebellar peduncle (input from spinal cord/brainstem)

front: What structures make up the limbic system?
back: Hippocampus, Amygdala, Parahippocampal gyrus, Cingulate gyrus, Hypothalamus, Mammillary bodies, Fornix

front: What is the pathway of the dorsal column-medial lemniscal (DCML) system?
back: Peripheral nerve → Dorsal root ganglion → Dorsal column (fasciculus gracilis/cuneatus) → Medulla → Decussate as internal arcuate fibers → Medial lemniscus → VPL nucleus of thalamus → Primary somatosensory cortex

front: What is the pathway of the lateral spinothalamic tract?
back: Peripheral nerve → Dorsal root ganglion → Rexed laminae I-V (substance substantia gelatinosa) → Decussate at spinal cord level → Ascend as lateral spinothalamic tract → VPL of thalamus → Primary somatosensory cortex

front: What are the 12 cranial nerves in order?
back: I Olfactory, II Optic, III Oculomotor, IV Trochlear, V Trigeminal, VI Abducens, VII Facial, VIII Vestibulocochlear, IX Glossopharyngeal, X Vagus, XI Accessory, XII Hypoglossal

front: Which cranial nerve carries parasympathetic fibers to the pupil (constriction)?
back: III Oculomotor nerve (Edinger-Westphal nucleus)

front: Which cranial nerves are involved in swallowing?
back: V (trigeminal - mastication), VII (facial - taste), IX (glossopharyngeal - sensation), X (vagus - motor)

front: What is the resting membrane potential of a neuron?
back: -70 mV (range: -60 to -80 mV)

front: What ion channels are responsible for the rising phase of an action potential?
back: Voltage-gated sodium channels (NaV1.1-1.9)

front: What ion channels are responsible for the falling phase of an action potential?
back: Voltage-gated potassium channels (KV1.1-1.4, KV7.x)

front: What type of synapse is most common in the CNS?
back: Chemical synapse (asymmetric Gray Type I or symmetric Gray Type II)

front: What is the mechanism of neurotransmitter release?
back: Action potential → Ca2+ influx via voltage-gated Ca2+ channels → Vesicle fusion → NT release into synaptic cleft → Receptor binding → Postsynaptic potential
